text: The surgeon infiltrates the surgical site with a local anesthetic and vasoconstrictor, visibly reducing blood flow to create a bloodless operative field.

The surgeon places a Lone Star retractor after making a skin incision to open the wound. Dissection is performed using scissors or a cold scalpel to minimize tissue damage. The torn sphincter edges are identified and gently mobilized, ensuring the scar tissue remains intact while avoiding damage to the anoderm or vaginal mucosa.

The surgeon mobilizes and blocks the edges of the sphincter without separately identifying the internal and external sphincters. Fibrotic tissue is left intact, and the full-length mobilization of the sphincter's fibrotic ends is performed up to the levator muscle if necessary. Careful lateral dissection is carried out to avoid damaging the anal nerves located at the three and nine o'clock positions, ensuring the muscle edges can overlap by at least two centimeters.
The surgeon begins muscle suturing by placing overlapping U-stitches using absorbable monofilament, carefully tying knots to prevent ischemic damage to the muscle tissue.
The surgeon considers adding levatorplasty, a procedure involving the levator muscle, to the surgical plan.
The surgeon closes the skin wound vertically, enhancing the newly created perineal body. No drainage is typically required in this step.
